questions.stem: A subset $I$ of a ring $R$ is an ideal when $I$ is an additive subgroup and additionally satisfies which absorption property for all $r \in R$ and $a \in I$?
questions.explanation: An ideal must absorb multiplication by every ring element from both sides: for all $r \in R$ and $a \in I$, both $ra \in I$ and $ar \in I$. This absorption (together with being an additive subgroup) is exactly what makes the coset multiplication on $R/I$ well defined.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The ideal test says a nonempty subset $I$ of a ring $R$ is an ideal if and only if it is closed under subtraction and under which multiplication?
options: Multiplication of an element of $I$ by any element of $R$ (on both sides).
Multiplication of any two elements of $I$.
questions.explanation: The ideal test requires closure under subtraction (for the additive subgroup) and that $ra$ and $ar$ lie in $I$ whenever $a \in I$ and $r \in R$. Closure of products of two elements of $I$ alone would only give a subring, not an ideal.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the factor ring $R/I$, how is the product of cosets $(a + I)$ and $(b + I)$ defined?
questions.explanation: Coset multiplication in $R/I$ is $(a + I)(b + I) = ab + I$, and coset addition is $(a + I) + (b + I) = (a + b) + I$. These operations are well defined precisely because $I$ is an ideal, so the result does not depend on representatives.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which of the following is an ideal of the ring $\mathbb{Z}$?
questions.explanation: The set $6\mathbb{Z}$ is an additive subgroup, and multiplying any multiple of $6$ by an arbitrary integer gives another multiple of $6$, so it absorbs multiplication. Hence $6\mathbb{Z}$ is an ideal. The other sets are not even additive subgroups of $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Every ideal of the ring $\mathbb{Z}$ has which form?
options: The set of all primes times $\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is a principal ideal domain: every ideal is $n\mathbb{Z}$ for some $n \ge 0$, generated by a single element $n$. This includes $\{0\} = 0\mathbb{Z}$ and $\mathbb{Z} = 1\mathbb{Z}$ as special cases.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In a commutative ring $R$ with unity, the principal ideal $\langle a \rangle$ generated by $a$ is which set?
questions.explanation: In a commutative ring with unity, $\langle a \rangle = \{ ra : r \in R \} = Ra$, the set of all multiples of $a$. It is the smallest ideal containing $a$. The set $\{ r : ra = 0 \}$ is instead the annihilator of $a$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The factor ring $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$ is isomorphic to which ring?
questions.explanation: The cosets of $6\mathbb{Z}$ are indexed by remainders modulo $6$, and coset arithmetic matches arithmetic modulo $6$, so $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_6$. (It happens to also be ring-isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_2 \times \mathbb{Z}_3$, but it is not a field since $\mathbb{Z}_6$ has zero divisors.)
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A proper ideal $M$ of a commutative ring $R$ with unity is maximal if and only if which factor ring is a field?
questions.explanation: For a commutative ring with unity, $M$ is a maximal ideal if and only if the factor ring $R/M$ is a field. Maximality of $M$ corresponds exactly to $R/M$ having no nontrivial proper ideals, which for a commutative ring with unity means it is a field.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: A proper ideal $P$ of a commutative ring $R$ with unity is prime if and only if which factor ring is an integral domain?
questions.explanation: $P$ is a prime ideal if and only if $R/P$ is an integral domain. Equivalently, $ab \in P$ forces $a \in P$ or $b \in P$, which is exactly the no-zero-divisors condition in the quotient.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: For the ring $\mathbb{Z}$, the ideal $\langle 5 \rangle = 5\mathbb{Z}$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Since $5$ is prime, $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_5$ is a field, so $\langle 5 \rangle$ is a maximal ideal. Every maximal ideal is prime, consistent with $\mathbb{Z}_5$ also being an integral domain. (Note: an ideal cannot be maximal without being prime.)
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the ring $\mathbb{Z}$, is the ideal $\langle 6 \rangle = 6\mathbb{Z}$ a prime ideal?
options: No, because $\langle 6 \rangle$ is not an ideal.
No, because $2 \cdot 3 \in \langle 6 \rangle$ but neither $2$ nor $3$ is in $\langle 6 \rangle$.
Yes, because it is an ideal.
questions.explanation: A prime ideal requires that $ab \in P$ implies $a \in P$ or $b \in P$. Here $2 \cdot 3 = 6 \in \langle 6 \rangle$, yet $2 \notin 6\mathbb{Z}$ and $3 \notin 6\mathbb{Z}$. So $\langle 6 \rangle$ is not prime, matching the fact that $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_6$ has zero divisors.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: How many elements does the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}_{12} / \langle 4 \rangle$ have, where $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8\}$?
questions.explanation: The ideal $\langle 4 \rangle = \{0, 4, 8\}$ has $3$ elements, so the factor ring has $12/3 = 4$ cosets. They are $\langle 4 \rangle$, $1 + \langle 4 \rangle$, $2 + \langle 4 \rangle$, and $3 + \langle 4 \rangle$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which implication between prime and maximal ideals is always true in a commutative ring with unity?
options: Prime and maximal ideals are the same thing.
questions.explanation: Every maximal ideal is prime, because a field is in particular an integral domain, so $R/M$ being a field forces it to be a domain. The converse fails: in $\mathbb{Z}$ the ideal $\{0\}$ is prime (since $\mathbb{Z}$ is a domain) but not maximal.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the ring $\mathbb{Z}$, the zero ideal $\{0\}$ is which of the following?
questions.explanation: Since $\mathbb{Z}/\{0\} \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is an integral domain, $\{0\}$ is a prime ideal. But $\mathbb{Z}$ is not a field, so $\{0\}$ is not maximal. This is the standard example of a prime ideal that is not maximal.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the identity element (multiplicative) of the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ when $n > 1$?
questions.explanation: The multiplicative identity of $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ is the coset $1 + n\mathbb{Z}$, since $(1 + n\mathbb{Z})(a + n\mathbb{Z}) = a + n\mathbb{Z}$ for every coset. The unity of a factor ring is the coset of the unity of $R$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The kernel of a ring homomorphism $\phi : R \to S$ is always which kind of subset of $R$?
options: A subring that is not an ideal.
questions.explanation: The kernel of a ring homomorphism is always an ideal of the domain $R$: it is an additive subgroup, and for $k \in \ker\phi$ and $r \in R$, $\phi(rk) = \phi(r)\phi(k) = \phi(r)\cdot 0 = 0$, so $rk \in \ker\phi$ (and similarly $kr$). This is the ring analogue of kernels being normal subgroups.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: If $I$ is an ideal of $R$ with $1 \in I$ (the unity), what must $I$ equal?
questions.explanation: If the unity $1$ lies in an ideal $I$, then for every $r \in R$ we have $r = r \cdot 1 \in I$ by absorption, so $I = R$. Thus a proper ideal can never contain a unit; in particular it cannot contain $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The First Isomorphism Theorem for rings states that for a homomorphism $\phi : R \to S$, which isomorphism holds?
questions.explanation: Just as for groups, the ring First Isomorphism Theorem gives $R/\ker\phi \cong \phi(R)$, where $\phi(R)$ is the image subring of $S$. The map $r + \ker\phi \mapsto \phi(r)$ is a well-defined ring isomorphism onto the image.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: For which value of $n$ is the ideal $\langle n \rangle = n\mathbb{Z}$ a maximal ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$?
questions.explanation: The ideal $n\mathbb{Z}$ is maximal exactly when $n$ is prime, because then $\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_n$ is a field. For composite $n$ the quotient has zero divisors (not even a domain), $n = 0$ gives the non-maximal prime $\{0\}$, and $n = 1$ gives all of $\mathbb{Z}$, which is not a proper ideal.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}_{12} / \langle 3 \rangle$, where $\langle 3 \rangle = \{0, 3, 6, 9\}$, how many elements are there?
questions.explanation: The ideal $\langle 3 \rangle = \{0, 3, 6, 9\}$ has $4$ elements, so the factor ring has $12/4 = 3$ cosets: $\langle 3 \rangle$, $1 + \langle 3 \rangle$, and $2 + \langle 3 \rangle$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly distinguishes a subring from an ideal?
options: An ideal must absorb multiplication by all ring elements, while a subring need only be closed under its own multiplication.
Every subring is an ideal, but not conversely.
Subrings are always larger than ideals.
questions.explanation: Both are additive subgroups closed under multiplication of their own elements, but an ideal has the stronger absorption property $ra, ar \in I$ for all $r \in R$. So every ideal is a subring, but not every subring is an ideal. For example $\mathbb{Z}$ is a subring of $\mathbb{Q}$ but not an ideal of $\mathbb{Q}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: What is the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}/7\mathbb{Z}$, and what algebraic structure is it?
questions.explanation: Since $7$ is prime, $\mathbb{Z}/7\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_7$ is a field: every nonzero coset is invertible. This matches the theorem that $\langle 7 \rangle$ is a maximal ideal of $\mathbb{Z}$, so the quotient is a field.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: The sum of two ideals $I$ and $J$ of a ring $R$, defined as $I + J = \{ a + b : a \in I, b \in J \}$, is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The sum $I + J$ is always an ideal of $R$: it is an additive subgroup, and absorbing multiplication on each summand shows $r(a + b) = ra + rb \in I + J$. In fact $I + J$ is the smallest ideal containing both $I$ and $J$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the ring $\mathbb{Z}$, which chain of ideals is correct (using $\subseteq$ for containment)?
questions.explanation: Every multiple of $4$ is also a multiple of $2$, so $4\mathbb{Z} \subseteq 2\mathbb{Z}$. In $\mathbb{Z}$, the containment $\langle a \rangle \subseteq \langle b \rangle$ holds exactly when $b$ divides $a$; since $2 \mid 4$, the smaller ideal is $4\mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Consider the ideal $I = \langle 5 \rangle = \{0, 5, 10, 15\}$ in the ring $\mathbb{Z}_{20}$. Find the number of elements in the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}_{20}/I$, and decide whether that factor ring is a field.
questions.solution: The ideal $I$ has $4$ elements, so the factor ring has $20/4$ cosets, that is {{1}} elements. This factor ring is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_5$, which since $5$ is prime is {{2}} a field.
questions.explanation: Since $|I| = 4$, the factor ring has $20/4 = 5$ elements and is isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_5$. As $5$ is prime, $\mathbb{Z}_5$ is indeed a field, so $I$ is a maximal ideal of $\mathbb{Z}_{20}$.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: In the ring $\mathbb{Z}$, consider the ideal $\langle 4 \rangle = 4\mathbb{Z}$. Find the number of elements in the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$, and state whether $\langle 4 \rangle$ is a prime ideal.
questions.solution: The cosets of $4\mathbb{Z}$ correspond to remainders modulo $4$, so $\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z}$ has {{1}} elements. Since $2 \cdot 2 = 4 \in \langle 4 \rangle$ while $2 \notin \langle 4 \rangle$, the ideal $\langle 4 \rangle$ is {{2}} prime.
questions.explanation: The factor ring $\mathbb{Z}/4\mathbb{Z} \cong \mathbb{Z}_4$ has $4$ elements. Because $4$ is composite, $\langle 4 \rangle$ is not prime: $2 \cdot 2 \in \langle 4 \rangle$ but $2 \notin \langle 4 \rangle$, and indeed $\mathbb{Z}_4$ has zero divisors.
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Work in the ring $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ with the ideal $I = \langle 6 \rangle = \{0, 6, 12\}$. Find the order of $I$ and the number of elements in the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}_{18}/I$.
questions.solution: The ideal $\langle 6 \rangle$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{18}$ consists of $\{0, 6, 12\}$, so its order is {{1}}. The factor ring then has $18/3$ cosets, that is {{2}} elements.
questions.explanation: The ideal $\langle 6 \rangle = \{0,6,12\}$ has order $3$, so the factor ring $\mathbb{Z}_{18}/\langle 6 \rangle$ has $18/3 = 6$ elements (isomorphic to $\mathbb{Z}_6$).
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra

questions.stem: Among the ideals $\langle 2 \rangle$, $\langle 8 \rangle$, and $\langle 11 \rangle$ of $\mathbb{Z}$, recall that $\langle n \rangle$ is maximal exactly when $n$ is prime. Count how many of these three are maximal ideals, and state whether $\langle 8 \rangle$ is maximal.
questions.solution: An ideal $\langle n \rangle$ is maximal in $\mathbb{Z}$ when $n$ is prime; among $2, 8, 11$ the primes are $2$ and $11$, so the number of maximal ideals is {{1}}. Since $8$ is not prime, $\langle 8 \rangle$ is {{2}} maximal.
questions.explanation: Among $2, 8, 11$, the primes are $2$ and $11$, giving $2$ maximal ideals $\langle 2 \rangle$ and $\langle 11 \rangle$. Since $8$ is composite, $\langle 8 \rangle$ is not maximal (the quotient $\mathbb{Z}_8$ is not a field).
questions.source: in the style of Gallian, Contemporary Abstract Algebra
